options: Yes — truth tables settle every case
No — Church and Turing proved that validity in first-order logic is undecidable
Yes — Gödel's completeness theorem makes it decidable
Only if the domain is finite
hint: Propositional logic is decidable; adding quantifiers over an arbitrary domain changes things.
explanation: First-order validity is UNDECIDABLE (Church's theorem, 1936, via Turing's halting problem) — no algorithm can decide validity for every FOL formula. FOL is, however, complete (Gödel) and semi-decidable: valid formulas can be enumerated, but there is no general procedure that also rejects every invalid one. Propositional logic, by contrast, is decidable.
